summary: Gruber responds to W3C CSS co-chairman Daniel Glazman's criticism that Apple's iBooks Author uses a proprietary file format instead of ePub standards. He argues that standards compliance hasn't helped Apple gain e-book market share, while Amazon's proprietary Kindle format dominates. Apple isn't claiming iBooks Author output is ePub — it's a proprietary format like Pages or Keynote, designed to be better today rather than waiting for standards bodies. Gruber frames this as Apple prioritizing competitive differentiation and user experience over open standards, drawing a parallel to the App Store's closed ecosystem. He concludes that ePub is to the new iBooks format as mobile web apps are to native iOS apps.
key_points: Amazon's proprietary Kindle format leads the market while Apple's standards-compliant ePub support hasn't made iBooks dominant — evidence contradicts Glazman's argument that openness would give Apple an advantage
Apple isn't corrupting ePub with proprietary extensions the way browser wars polluted HTML; iBooks Author is its own format, like Pages or Keynote
The proprietary format exists to ship superior layout features today rather than waiting for W3C approval cycles
Apple's priorities remain consistent: Apple first, users second, developers third — publishers must choose between iBooks exclusivity, painful cross-platform workflows, or inferior tools
iBooks still supports standard ePub, making the situation analogous to web apps versus native iOS apps — open standard supported alongside a superior closed platform
money_quotes: It's worth noting that the e-book market leader, Amazon's Kindle, uses a proprietary format. Eschewing ePub and any sort of standards compliance doesn't seem to have hurt Amazon.
It's the difference between 'What's the best we can do within the constraints of the current ePub spec?' versus 'What's the best we can do given the constraints of our engineering talent?'
One man's nonstandard is another man's competitive differentiation.
Apple's concern is not what's best for the publishing industry, and it certainly isn't about what's best for the makers of (and users of) rival e-book reading devices.

The iBooks format isn't different just for the sake of being different; it's different for the sake of being better — not better in the future, after a W3C review period and approval, but better today.
It's worth noting that the app's name is iBooks Author, not eBooks Author.

I see ePub being as to the new iBooks format as mobile web apps are to native iOS App Store apps — one is an open industry standard fully supported by Apple, the other a closed proprietary platform with superior creation tools and end-user experience.
key_insight: Apple chose proprietary formats for iBooks Author not out of hostility to standards but because shipping superior layout features today matters more than waiting for standards bodies to catch up — and the market evidence suggests proprietary formats win anyway.
